channel.title: Nature
channel.description: Nature is the foremost international weekly scientific journal in the world and is the flagship journal for Nature Portfolio. It publishes the finest peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nature publishes landmark papers, award winning news, leading comment and expert opinion on important, topical scientific news and events that enable readers to share the latest discoveries in science and evolve the discussion amongst the global scientific community.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Why do curling stones slide across ice the way they do?
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 20 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00556-2

After a century of study, physicists have theories — but they’re still not 100% sure.
item.dc:title: Why do curling stones slide across ice the way they do?
item.dc:creator: Jenna Ahart

item.title: Giant energy storage and dielectric performance in all-polymer nanocomposites
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 18 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10195-2

A high-temperature immiscible blend of two dipolar polymers that self-assemble into three-dimensional all-polymer nanocomposites allows markedly enhanced dielectric and energy storage performances over a broad temperature range.
item.dc:title: Giant energy storage and dielectric performance in all-polymer nanocomposites
item.dc:creator: Li Li
Guanchun Rui
Wenyi Zhu
Yiwen Guo
Zitan Huang
Siyu Wu

Ralph H. Colby
Seong H. Kim
Wenchang Lu
J. Bernholc
Q. M. Zhang

item.title: Practical lithium–organic batteries enabled by an n-type conducting polymer
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 18 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10174-7

An n-type conducting polymer with excellent mixed ionic and electronic transport enables a practical lithium–organic battery that exhibits a wide operating temperature range and excellent safety.
item.dc:title: Practical lithium–organic batteries enabled by an n-type conducting polymer
item.dc:creator: Zhenfei Li

Fei Huang

item.title: Laser writing in glass for dense, fast and efficient archival data storage
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 18 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-025-10042-w

An optical archival storage technology based on femtosecond laser direct writing in glass addresses the practical demands of archival storage.
item.dc:title: Laser writing in glass for dense, fast and efficient archival data storage

item.title: Integrated photonics enabling ultra-wideband fibre–wireless communication
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 18 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10172-9

An integrated photonics scheme is presented for the manufacture of communication systems supporting the use of fibre and wireless infrastructures simultaneously, addressing the long-standing bandwidth mismatch between the two domains and demonstrating ultrahigh data rates.
item.dc:title: Integrated photonics enabling ultra-wideband fibre–wireless communication
item.dc:creator: Yunhao Zhang
Haowen Shu

item.title: Higher-dimensional Fermiology in bulk moiré metals
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 18 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10173-8

Aperiodic composite crystals were discovered that emulate 2D moiré materials, demonstrating a potentially scalable approach for producing moiré materials for next-generation electronics and a generalizable approach for realizing theoretical predictions of higher-dimensional quantum phenomena.
item.dc:title: Higher-dimensional Fermiology in bulk moiré metals
item.dc:creator: Kevin P. Nuckolls
Nisarga Paul
Alan Chen
Filippo Gaggioli
Joshua P. Wakefield
Avi Auslender
Jules Gardener
Austin J. Akey
David Graf
Takehito Suzuki
David C. Bell
Liang Fu
Joseph G. Checkelsky

item.title: Cold-injection synthesis of highly emissive perovskite nanocrystals
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 18 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10117-2

A cold-injection method based on pseudo-emulsion enables scalable synthesis of stable, pure-green perovskite nanocrystals with near-unity photoluminescence quantum yield, achieved through defect-suppressing slow polybromide plumbate assembly at cold temperatures.
item.dc:title: Cold-injection synthesis of highly emissive perovskite nanocrystals
item.dc:creator: Sungjin Kim

item.title: Microsoft team creates ‘revolutionary’ data-storage system that lasts for millennia
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 18 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00502-2

Researchers use mini plasma explosions to encode the equivalent of two million books into a coaster-sized device. The method could preserve research data for millennia with minimal storage costs.
item.dc:title: Microsoft team creates ‘revolutionary’ data-storage system that lasts for millennia
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Gibney

item.title: Chip-scale device efficiently boosts light signals
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 18 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00484-1

Optical-amplifier devices that boost light signals are workhorses of modern technology, but shrinking them onto microchips requires impractically high power. An amplifier architecture has been developed that recycles its own ‘pump’ energy to achieve high performance at power levels ten times lower than those of previous designs, making low-power, chip-scale optical amplification practical.
item.dc:title: Chip-scale device efficiently boosts light signals

item.title: Laser-written glass tablets can preserve data for millennia
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 18 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00286-5

An archive system that encodes information in modified optical properties of glass could be a fast, efficient way to store huge quantities of data.
item.dc:title: Laser-written glass tablets can preserve data for millennia
item.dc:creator: Feng Chen
Bo Wu
